does: This paper extends transcoder-based circuit tracing to multimodal diffusion transformers, specifically targeting FLUX.1[schnell]. The core contribution is training timestep-conditioned transcoders that faithfully approximate MLP sublayer behavior in image generation models, enabling researchers to replace MLPs with these interpretable components and linearize remaining computations. The method reconstructs how semantic information propagates across denoising steps and reveals interactions between text and image representations within double-stream multimodal DiT architectures. By decomposing these complex nonlinear transformations into interpretable features and their compositions, the approach provides mechanistic understanding of how diffusion models generate images conditioned on text prompts.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in transcoder-based circuit tracing developed for large language models, extending techniques that have proven effective for causal analysis in autoregressive transformers to the more challenging setting of multimodal generative models. It addresses acknowledged limitations of prior interpretability approaches like attention visualization and sparse autoencoders, which offer incomplete views of model computation. The paper responds to the emerging gap in mechanistic interpretability between well-studied language models and less understood multimodal diffusion models. It leverages the conceptual framework of feature decomposition and circuit analysis while innovating the application to timestep-conditioned architectures, where understanding computation at each denoising step adds crucial temporal complexity.
enables: This work opens pathways for detailed causal analysis of large-scale image generation models, enabling researchers to trace how specific semantic concepts flow through denoising processes and how text-image fusion occurs within multimodal architectures. Future work could apply similar methods to understand failure modes, guide model improvements, or investigate how conflicting text descriptions are resolved during generation. The transcoder-based approach could be extended to other diffusion variants, other timestep-conditioned architectures, or to study how different denoising schedules affect feature transformations. Understanding these mechanisms may eventually support controllable generation, bias mitigation, or adversarial robustness in large-scale generative models.
